reason: Three.js renders five clearly distinct, labeled spheres (chrome metal, transmissive glass, matte rubber, glowing emissive, glossy clearcoat) with soft contact shadows, visible softboxes, and RoomEnvironment-based reflections — production quality, screenshot and metrics agree. Aura3D's metrics also report success (compiles, runs, no hallucinated APIs, no timeout), but its screenshot contradicts this: a dark, poorly framed scene with only ~4 spheres clearly visible and content cut off, where the five required material types do not read as visually distinct (no clear chrome metal or glass transmission; pink/teal swatches read as plain colored). Aura's reliance on prefabs.materialSwatches() produced a non-production result, so the hard prompt is not satisfied. Modularity is comparable: Aura is concise and declarative (138 lines) but hides materials behind a prefab; Three.js is longer (260 lines) but well-organized with data-driven sampleData and helper functions. No timeout or nontermination in either run.
failedThresholds: Aura3D: five spheres not all clearly visible (only ~4 readable, content cut off)
Aura3D: five material types not visually distinct
Aura3D: not production quality (hard prompt 07 fail)
